title: Can China Build a Self-Sufficient Semiconductor Equipment Supply Chain Without ASML?
gist: A short forum discussion-starter citing Nikkei/Newswitch reports that ASML's China sales share is declining while Chinese equipment makers (SMEE, NAURA, AMEC) advance at mature nodes, asking whether China can reach near self-sufficiency in chip manufacturing without EUV.

insights.claim: Per Nikkei and Newswitch, ASML's share of sales in China is declining while Chinese semiconductor-equipment companies rapidly expand capabilities.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A falling China share is a structural headwind for ASML's mature-node tool revenue as domestic substitution accelerates.
insights.quote: ASML's share of sales in China has continued to decline, while Chinese semiconductor equipment companies are rapidly expanding their capabilities.
insights.locator: According to recent industry reports from Nikkei and Newswitch...

insights.claim: Reported Chinese milestones include SMEE validating 28nm ArF immersion lithography tools, NAURA mass-producing 28nm equipment, and AMEC validating tools for 14nm production at SMIC.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Mature-node (28nm/14nm) domestic toolchain progress is the substitution front to watch; advanced lithography (EUV) remains the unmet gap.
insights.quote: SMEE reportedly validating 28nm ArF immersion lithography tools

insights.claim: The post frames the open question of whether mature-node manufacturing (28nm/14nm and above) can satisfy most domestic Chinese demand, or whether advanced-node dependence remains a major bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The mature-vs-advanced node question is the core uncertainty for sizing China's self-sufficiency timeline and ASML's exposure.
insights.quote: Will mature-node manufacturing (28nm, 14nm, and above) be enough to support most domestic demand, or will advanced-node dependence remain a major bottleneck?
